threats.title: Uncapped TGT Deposits Lead to TITN Insolvency and Permanent DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt.sol does not enforce a maximum cap on TGT deposits. If the total TGT deposited exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE, the accumulated totalTitnClaimable will exceed the TITN_ARB balance deposited by the admin. This causes insolvency, preventing late claimers from withdrawing and permanently bricking withdrawRemainingTitn() due to an arithmetic underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract assumes TGT deposits will not exceed a certain threshold but fails to enforce it in code. Validating this ensures the protocol remains solvent under unexpected deposit volumes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No upper bound check on totalTitnClaimable or deposited TGT amount

preconditions: Total TGT deposited by users exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579,000,000 TGT)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: totalTitnClaimable can grow larger than the contract's actual TITN balance

analysis_questions: Can an attacker acquire enough TGT to push the contract into insolvency?
Does the underflow in withdrawRemainingTitn permanently lock all post-year claims?
validation_requirements: Verify claimTitn reverts for late claimers
Verify withdrawRemainingTitn reverts due to underflow

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker or group of users deposits more than 579M TGT. The contract allocates more TITN than it holds. Early claimers drain the TITN balance. Late claimers get nothing. After 360 days, withdrawRemainingTitn underflows and reverts for everyone.
prerequisites: Availability of TGT tokens exceeding the expected cap

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of funds for late claimers and permanent lock of all post-year TITN
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who claim late or wait for the post-year proportional distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin to deposit additional TITN to restore solvency

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for cumulative deposits exceeding the expected maximum

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: High TGT capital required, but can be achieved organically by users

threats.title: Cross-Chain Tainting Attack Permanently Locks Victim's TITN Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: The _credit function in Titn.sol permanently sets isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true for any recipient of a cross-chain transfer. An attacker can bridge a dust amount of TITN to any arbitrary address on Base, permanently flagging the victim. If bridged transfers are locked, the victim cannot transfer any TITN they own.
threats.investigation_rationale: The permanent, un-clearable nature of the isBridgedTokenHolder flag creates a severe griefing vector where an attacker can remotely freeze a victim's assets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State flag set permanently based on untrusted cross-chain input

preconditions: isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true on the destination chain (Base)

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to clear the flag or opt-out of receiving bridged tokens

analysis_questions: Can the attacker specify an arbitrary recipient address in the LayerZero send() call?
Does the restriction apply to natively minted tokens held by the victim?
validation_requirements: Simulate bridging 1 wei to a victim address
Verify victim's subsequent transfer() calls revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls send() on Arbitrum, bridging 1 wei of TITN to a victim's address on Base. The victim's isBridgedTokenHolder flag is set to true. The victim is now unable to transfer or sell their natively acquired TITN on Base.
prerequisites: Ability to pay LayerZero cross-chain fees for a dust transfer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim's TITN tokens are permanently frozen until admin lifts the global lock
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any targeted TITN holder on Base

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for unsolicited cross-chain transfers to arbitrary addresses

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time while lock is active
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via LZ messages to unexpected addresses

threats.title: Misleading LZ Endpoint Exemption Causes Permanent Loss of Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: Titn.sol restricts transfers on Arbitrum but exempts transfers where to == lzEndpoint, intending to force users to bridge. However, a standard ERC20 transfer to the endpoint does not trigger a cross-chain message; it traps the tokens in the endpoint contract. Actual bridging uses send() which bypasses transfer() entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly carves out an exemption that encourages users to perform an action (transfer to endpoint) that results in permanent loss of funds, demonstrating a fundamental misunderstanding of OFT mechanics.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Exemption for to != lzEndpoint in transfer validation

preconditions: User attempts to bridge tokens by calling transfer(lzEndpoint, amount)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allowing direct transfers to the LZ endpoint contract
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should block all transfers and only allow OFT send() via _burn

analysis_questions: Does transferring tokens to the LZ endpoint trigger any bridging logic?
Are tokens recoverable from the LZ endpoint contract?
validation_requirements: Confirm tokens remain stuck in the endpoint contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user reads the documentation or contract code, sees that transfers to the LZ endpoint are allowed, and calls transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) expecting their tokens to bridge to Base. The tokens are permanently stuck in the endpoint contract.
prerequisites: User interaction based on misleading contract logic

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of transferred tokens for the user
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users attempting to bridge manually via transfer
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, tokens stuck in LZ endpoint

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing end-to-end test verifying that direct transfers to the endpoint do not bridge tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via TITN balance accumulating in the LZ endpoint address

threats.title: Off-By-One Boundary Condition Causes Irreversible Loss of TGT
threats.hypothesis_description: At exactly 360 days after launch, onTokenTransfer allows TGT deposits because it checks `> 360 days`. However, quoteTitn returns 0 because it checks `< 360 days`. A user depositing at exactly this timestamp will have their TGT taken but receive 0 claimable TITN, and cannot claim because claimTitn reverts at `>= 360 days`.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inconsistent boundary checks across related functions create a narrow window where state transitions are destructive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Inconsistent boundary operators (> vs < vs >=) across functions

preconditions: User deposits TGT in the exact block where block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days

analysis_questions: Can a transaction land exactly on the 360-day boundary?
Does the ERC677 transferAndCall execute before the 0-value return is processed?
validation_requirements: Set block.timestamp to exactly launchTime + 360 days
Execute onTokenTransfer and verify TGT is taken but claimable TITN is 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user's deposit transaction is mined in a block exactly 360 days after launch. The contract accepts the TGT via transferAndCall, but quoteTitn returns 0. The user receives 0 claimable TITN and permanently loses their TGT.
prerequisites: Exact block timestamp matching the 360-day boundary

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of deposited TGT for the affected user
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users depositing at the exact boundary

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge-case tests for exact timestamp boundaries

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via TGT transfer event with 0 claimable TITN credited

threats.title: Proportional Share Calculation Truncates Dust, Leaving TITN Permanently Locked
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn, userProportionalShare is calculated using integer division. Due to truncation, the sum of all users' proportional shares will be strictly less than unclaimedTitn. The residual dust TITN will remain permanently locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Precision loss in proportional distribution mechanisms often leads to permanently locked funds if there is no sweep mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Integer division in proportional distribution without remainder tracking

preconditions: claimableTitn * unclaimedTitn is not perfectly divisible by initialTotalClaimable

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to sweep remaining dust after all users have claimed

analysis_questions: How much dust can accumulate in the worst-case scenario?
Is there any way to extract the locked dust?
validation_requirements: Simulate multiple users claiming with prime number balances
Verify contract balance > 0 after all claims are processed

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Normal protocol operation occurs. After 360 days, all users call withdrawRemainingTitn. Due to division truncation, a small amount of TITN remains in the contract. Since remainingTitnAfter1Year is already initialized, no one can claim this dust.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Small amount of TITN permanently locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can use withdraw() to extract it

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying contract balance is exactly 0 after all claims

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via residual balance in contract

threats.title: Owner Can Drain User-Allocated TITN via Unrestricted Withdraw
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function in MergeTgt.sol allows the owner to transfer any amount of any ERC20 token out of the contract, including the TITN tokens backing users' claimable balances. This breaks the solvency invariant and allows a rug pull.
threats.investigation_rationale: Privileged functions that can arbitrarily drain user funds violate core trust assumptions, even if the owner is currently trusted.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unrestricted admin withdrawal of protocol assets

preconditions: Owner account is malicious or compromised

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Owner can withdraw TITN needed for user claims

analysis_questions: Does the withdraw function bypass all accounting logic?
What happens to user claims if the balance is drained?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised or malicious owner calls withdraw(titn, balance). The contract is drained. Users attempting to call claimTitn or withdrawRemainingTitn experience transaction reverts due to insufficient balance or underflow.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all user-allocated TITN

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests ensuring owner cannot withdraw below totalTitnClaimable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via large owner withdrawal events
